Opening up a world of opportunity

HSBC is one of the largest banking and financial
services organisations in the world.

Guided by our purpose of opening up a world of
opportunity, our ambition is to become the world’s
most trusted bank globally, putting customers at
the heart of everything we do.

Who we are

Founded in 1865, HSBC is one of the world’s largest banking and financial services
organisations. We’re here to use our expertise, capabilities, breadth and
perspectives to help open up a world of opportunity for our customers.

Our strategy Our strategy supports our ambition to be the most trusted bank globally, putting customers at the heart of
        everything we do. We help meet our customers’ financial needs and support them to achieve their goals
        with our products and services, while navigating the complexities of the global market through our deep
        international network, supported with the stability and strength of our balance sheet.


    Our priorities      u Be simple           u Drive customer-                         u Deliver focused
                                          and agile                         centricity                     sustainable growth

    Ñ See page 9 for further details on   We aim to make fast, safe         We are intensely focused on    As a leading international bank,
    our strategy.                         decisions – adapting to change    our customers – helping to     we aim to drive long-term,
                                          by staying relevant, driving      deliver excellent outcomes,    sustainable growth, focused on
                                          simplification and being future   drive loyalty, and serve our   areas of competitive strength.
                                          ready through technology and      customers for the long term
                                          digitisation.                     through the depth of what we
                                                                            offer as a franchise.

    Our values

    At HSBC, our values guide us in all our actions – from strategic decisions to day-to-day interactions with customers and each other. Our values are
    rooted in HSBC’s history, heritage and character, and help us deliver on our purpose.

      We get it done                          We value difference     We take responsibility           We succeed together
      Moving at pace and making               Seeking out different   Holding ourselves accountable    Collaborating across
      things happen                           perspectives            and taking the long view         boundaries

Group Chairman’s shareholder letter



                                                      =                                                Today, HSBC is clear on its core strengths,
                                                                                                       investing to further develop our competitive
                                                                                                       advantages and deliver sustainable growth,
                                                                                                       with an entirely attainable ambition to be the
                                                                                                       most trusted bank globally, putting customers
        4                                                                                              at the heart of everything we do.

                                                                                                       Global Context
                                                                                                       Global growth in 2025 was stronger than
                                                                                                       expected, as the tariff-related headwinds were
                                                                                                       offset by the significant momentum generated
                                                                                                       by AI capital expenditure and trade growth,

                                                                                                       and by the support provided by the ever-
                                                                                                       resilient US consumer.

                                                      -                                                The global geopolitical context was marked by
                                                                                                       continued uncertainty. The war in Ukraine,
                                                                                                       which has entered its fifth year, and conflicts
                                                                                                       in the Middle East and elsewhere, continue to
                                                                                                       have significant human consequences.

                                                                                                       In parallel, the changing approach to global
                                                     =                                                 trade relations has increased economic
                                                                                                       uncertainty. But as the resilience of global
        -                                                                                              trade growth demonstrates, the inter-
                                                           “ZN                                         connectedness of the global economy,
                                                                                                       underpinned by growing trade flows, is
                                                           pf                                          compelling.
    Brendan Nelson                                                                                     Faced with the re-configuration of the
    Group Chairman                                                                                     globalised world, HSBC is optimally positioned
                                                                                                       to help our customers capture the meaningful
    We delivered strong performance and material returns for our                                       opportunities that are driving the global
    shareholders in 2025. By leveraging our unique global network and                                  economy forward, across geographies and
    leading capabilities, we helped our customers see past the sustained                               throughout our unique global network. Our
                                                                                                       strong financial performance and material
    uncertainty in the international environment and find the opportunities                            returns in 2025 point to that dynamic, along
    that are driving the global economy forward.                                                       with our focused approach to implementing
                                                                                                       our strategic priorities.

    Our strategy

    In 2025, we continued to implement our strategy that supports our ambition to be the most
    trusted bank globally, putting customers at the heart of everything we do.

ESG overview
Our approach to ESG is focused on creating long-term value for our customers
and wider stakeholders.

Our approach

Progress on our ESG metrics

We have established ambitions and targets that guide how we do business, including how we operate and how we serve our customers. We set out
below some of the key ESG metrics we use to measure progress against our ambitions. To help us achieve our ESG ambitions, a number of measures
are included in the incentive scorecards of the Group CEO, Group CFO and Group Operating Committee members that underpin some of the ESG
metrics in the table below. For a summary of how our non-financial metrics link to executive remuneration, see pages 220 - 223 of the Director’s
remuneration report.

    Risk overview



Top and emerging risks

Our top and emerging risks report identifies Group. We actively manage and take actions monitor closely the identified risks and agree
forward-looking risks so that they can be to mitigate our top risks. Emerging risks are management actions to remediate and/or
considered in determining whether any those that, while they could have a material reduce them to acceptable levels, as required.
incremental action is needed to either prevent impact on our risk profile were they to occur,
them from materialising or to limit their effect. are not considered immediate and are not Ñ For further detail on our top and emerging risks,
Top risks are those that have the potential to under active management. Our suite of top see page 100.
have a material adverse impact on the financial and emerging risks is subject to regular review
results, reputation or business model of the by senior governance forums. We continue to


 Risk Trend Description
 Externally driven
 Geopolitical and ~ Our operations and portfolios are subject to risks arising from political instability, civil unrest and military conflict, which may
 macroeconomic risks lead to disruption of our operations, physical risk to our staff and/or physical damage to our assets. We are also subject to
        macroeconomic risks, which may drive changes to our income growth and asset quality. Heightened geopolitical and
        macroeconomic risk globally, including uncertainty in international trade policy, is subject to close monitoring and review.
 Technology and ~ There is an increased risk of service disruption or loss of data resulting from technology failures or malicious activities from
 cybersecurity risk internal or external threats. We continue to monitor changes to the technology and threat landscape, including those arising
        from ongoing geopolitical and macroeconomic events alongside third-party incidents and the impact this may have on risk
        management. We operate a continuous improvement programme to help support the resilience and stability of our technology
        operations and counter a fast-evolving and heightened cyber threat environment.
 Environmental, social ~ We are subject to ESG risks, including in relation to climate change, nature and human rights. These risks have increased due
 and governance to diverging national and political agendas, a more complex and prescriptive regulatory environment across the jurisdictions
 (‘ESG’) risks we operate in, as well as increasing frequency of severe weather events across the globe. Financial institutions’ actions and
        investment decisions in respect of ESG matters continue to be subject to heightened scrutiny by stakeholders. Failure to meet
        these evolving expectations may have financial and non-financial impacts, including reputational, legal and regulatory
        compliance risks.
 Financial crime risk ~ We are exposed to financial crime risk from our customers, staff and third parties engaging in criminal activity. The financial
        crime risk environment is heightened due to increasingly complex geopolitical challenges, the macroeconomic outlook, the
        complex and dynamic nature of sanctions and export control compliance, evolving financial crime regulations, rapid
        technological developments, an increasing number of national data privacy requirements and the increasing sophistication of
        fraud. As a result, we will continue to face the possibility of regulatory enforcement and reputational risk.
 Digitalisation and ~ Developments in technology and changes in regulations continue to enable new entrants to the banking industry as well as
 technological new products and services offered by competitors. This challenges us to continue to innovate with new digital capabilities and
 advances risk evolve our products, to attract, retain and best serve our customers. Along with opportunities, new technology, including
        GenAI, can introduce risks and disruption. We seek to manage technology developments with appropriate controls and
        oversight.
 Evolving regulatory ~ The regulatory and compliance risks are set against continued geopolitical risk and regulatory focus on operational resilience,
 environment risk resolvability, prudential requirements, financial reporting and data, ESG, conduct, as well as sound risk and financial crime risk
        management practices. The approach to regulation is increasingly fragmented, including in relation to AI and digital assets,
        and a trend towards deregulation has emerged in some jurisdictions, concurrently with regulatory actions to support business
        growth.
 Internally driven
 Data risk } We use data to serve our customers and run our operations, often in real-time within digital experiences and processes. If our
        data is not accurate and timely, our ability to serve customers, operate with resilience or meet regulatory requirements could
        be impacted. We seek to ensure that non-public data is kept confidential, and that we comply with the growing number of
        regulations that govern data privacy and cross-border movement of data.
 Risks arising from the ~ We procure goods and services from a range of third parties. In the current macroeconomic and geopolitical climate, the risk
 receipt of services of service disruption in supply chains is elevated, driven by an industry-wide increase in supply chain cyber threats. We
 from third parties continue to strengthen our controls, oversight and risk management policies and processes to select and manage third parties,
        including our third parties’ own supply chains, particularly for key activities that could affect our operational resilience.
 Model risk } Model risk arises whenever business decision making includes reliance on models. We use models in both financial and non-
        financial contexts, as well as in a range of business applications. Evolving regulatory requirements and enhanced expectations
        continue to drive changes to the way model risk is managed across the banking industry, with a particular focus on capital and
        credit loss models. New technologies, including AI, are driving a need for enhanced model risk controls.
 Strategic execution ~ Successful execution of our strategy enables us to help address the swiftly changing needs of our customers and
 risk stakeholders. We are committed to enhancing the effectiveness of strategic execution risk controls and monitoring. This will
        help us minimise disruptions during a period of heightened execution risk, driven by the complexity and scale of ongoing
        strategic, regulatory and technological change.
 Risks associated with ~ Our businesses, functions and geographies are exposed to risks associated with employee retention and talent availability, the
 workforce capability, evolving skills requirements of our workforce, and compliance with employment laws and regulations. Voluntary attrition
 capacity and across the Group remains stable, but failure to manage these risks may impact the delivery of our strategic objectives or lead
 environmental factors to regulatory sanctions or legal claims, and the risks are heightened during the implementation of organisational change.
 with potential impact
 on growth

~ Risk heightened during 2025 } Risk remained at the same level as 2024

Transition to net zero
We aim to support the transition to net zero and a sustainable future
in partnership with our customers and other stakeholders

    Data and methodology limitations

Our financed emissions estimates and methodological choices are shaped by data availability for our sectors. We are members of the PCAF,
which defines and develops GHG accounting standards for financial institutions. Its Global GHG Accounting and Reporting Standards for
Financed Emissions and for Facilitated Emissions provide detailed methodological guidance.
– We have found that data quality scores vary – Most of our clients do not yet report the full – Reported client emissions data may have up
     across the different sectors and years of our   scope of GHG emissions included in our            to a two-year lag, which may result in
     analysis. While we expect our data quality      analysis, in particular scope 3 at a subsidiary   alignment challenges to financial reporting
     scores to improve over time, as companies       level. In the absence of client-reported          dates and lead to further reliance on proxies.
     continue to expand their disclosures to meet    emissions, we estimated emissions using          – Mapping external datasets to our internal
     growing regulatory and stakeholder              proxies based on company production and           client entities can be challenging due to
     expectations, there may be fluctuations         revenue figures. We applied industry              complex company ownership structures.
     within sectors year-on-year, and/or             averages in our analysis where company-          – The methodology and data used to assess
     differences in the data quality scores due to   specific data was unavailable, using third-       financed emissions and set targets continue
     changes in data availability.                   party datasets. As data improves for client-      to evolve and we expect industry guidance,
                                                     reported emissions, our reliance on               market practice, and regulations to continue
                                                     estimates will continue to reduce.                to change.

    1 Our absolute and intensity emissions metrics and targets are measured based on the drawn exposures of the counterparties in scope for each sector. Emissions
      intensity is a weighted average according to the portfolio weight of each investment, as a proportion of the total portfolio value.
    2 For the oil and gas sector, absolute emissions are measured in million tonnes of carbon dioxide equivalent (‘Mt CO2e’); for the power and utilities sector, intensity
      is measured in tonnes of carbon dioxide equivalent per gigawatt hour (‘tCO2e/GWh’); for the cement sector, intensity is measured in tonnes of carbon dioxide
      equivalent per tonne of cement (‘tCO2e/t cement’); for the iron and steel sector, intensity is measured in tonnes of carbon dioxide equivalent per tonne of steel
      (‘tCO2e/t steel’); for the aviation sector, intensity is measured in tonnes of carbon dioxide equivalent per million revenue tonne kilometres (‘tCO2e/million rtk’); for
      the automotive sector, intensity is measured in tonnes of carbon dioxide equivalent per million vehicle kilometres (‘tCO2e/million vkm’); and for the thermal coal
      mining sector, absolute emissions are measured in million tonnes of carbon dioxide equivalent (‘Mt CO2e’).
    3 We have changed our reporting unit for aviation from revenue passenger kilometre (‘rpk’) to revenue tonne kilometre (‘rtk’) to better align to counterparties in scope
      which often include all airline activities (passengers, belly cargo, dedicated cargo). Additionally, this metric enables direct comparison to climate scenarios that are based
      on traffic demand forecasts and aligns to industry practice.
    4 The thermal coal mining scope differs from the other target sectors. We include solely emissions from thermal coal production and coal power generation, rather than
      the total emissions of a counterparty within a sector, to reflect the thermal coal mining absolute financed emissions reduction target.

                                                         emissions-intensity clients.

    Managing climate risk TCFD

    Climate risk relates to the financial and non-      Asset management                                 The Scheme reports the carbon footprint for
    financial impacts that may arise as a result of     HSBC Asset Management recognises that            its equity and corporate bond mandates in its
    climate change and the move to a net zero           climate-related risks may impact the             annual TCFD Report, and will seek to widen
    economy. We manage climate risk across all          operational and financial performance of         the coverage of its assessment and reporting
    our businesses and incorporate climate              investee companies. The impact of these risks    over time. In line with the Trustee’s
    considerations within our traditional risk types,   will vary depending on characteristics such as   commitment to good stewardship, the Trustee
    in line with our Group-wide risk management         asset class, sector, business model and          engages its asset managers to seek to ensure
    framework.                                          geography. We continue to integrate climate      that financially material ESG risks are explicitly
    Our material exposure to climate risk relates to    analysis into our actively managed product       considered in the investment process.
    wholesale and retail client financing activity      offerings and seek to assess climate-related
    within our banking portfolio. We are also           risks that may impact investment                 Insurance
                                                                                                         exploratory solvency assessment of our
    exposed to climate risk in relation to asset        performance, where relevant.                     We are improving our ability to perform
    ownership by our insurance business and             As part of our stewardship activities, we        biggest insurance businesses under climate
    employee pension plans. Our clients are             engage on climate change issues with             stress scenarios.
    exposed to climate-related investment risk in       investee companies on a priority list, as
    our Asset Management business.                      defined in our Stewardship Plan. HSBC Asset      Ñ For further details of HSBC Asset Management’s

Building inclusion and resilience
We play an active role in opening up a world of opportunity for our customers,
colleagues and communities by connecting across our international networks
to help build a more inclusive and resilient society.

Our commitment to inclusion
Our approach

geographies and cultures.                   needs and deliver improved outcomes.             areas as detailed below.

    Customer experience

    We remain committed to improving customers’ experiences. In 2025, we gathered feedback from over one million customers across our four
    business segments to help us understand our strengths and the areas we need to focus on.

    Customer satisfaction

    We continue to listen, learn and act on            enterprises, and improved our SME Business     to 54 points, compared with 48 points in 2024.
    customer feedback. We use the net promoter         Banking ranking to fifth.
    score (‘NPS’) system to share feedback with        In IWPB, among the mass affluent we            In CIB, among Corporates we were ranked
    our front-line teams, allowing them to respond     improved our NPS or rank in seven of 10 key    among the top 3 in seven of 10 key markets.

    How we listen

    To improve how we serve our customers, we must be open to feedback and acknowledge when things go wrong. We continue to adapt at pace to
    provide support for customers facing new challenges, new ways of working and those that require enhanced care needs. We aim to be open and
    consistent in how we track, record and manage complaints, although as we serve a wide range of customers – from personal banking and wealth
    customers to large corporates, institutions and governments – we tailor our approach in each of our global businesses.

     How we handle complaints

     Our principles                  Our actions
     Making it easy for              Customers can complain through the channel that best suits them. We provide a point of contact along with
     customers to complain           clear information on next steps and timescales.
     Acknowledging complaints        All colleagues welcome complaints as opportunities and exercise empathy to acknowledge our customers’
                                     issues. Complaints are escalated if they cannot be resolved at first point of contact.
     Keeping the customer up to      We set clear expectations and keep customers informed throughout the complaint resolution process
     date                            through their preferred channel.
     Ensuring fair resolution        We thoroughly investigate all complaints to address concerns and ensure the right outcome for our
                                     customers.
     Providing available rights      We provide customers with information on their rights and the appeal process if they are not satisfied with
                                     the outcome of the complaint.
     Undertaking root cause          Complaint causes are analysed on a regular basis to identify and address any systemic issues and to inform
     analysis                        process improvements.

1  Gross interest yield is the average annualised interest rate earned on average interest-earning assets (‘AIEA’), net of amortised premiums and loan fees. Gross
   interest payable is the average annualised interest cost as a percentage of average interest-bearing liabilities.

   annualised interest rate payable on average interest-bearing funds.
3  Net interest margin is net interest income expressed as an annualised percentage of AIEA.

    Wealth balances
    The following table shows our wealth balances, which include invested assets and wealth deposits. Invested assets comprise customer assets
    either managed by our Asset Management business or by external third-party investment managers, as well as self-directed investments by our
    customers. From 1 January 2026, we have updated the definition of our wealth balances to exclude Asset Management third-party distribution.
    This will enhance comparability with industry peers.

    defined as a master group (HSBC’s own client groupings) that includes                 well as other non-client revenue.
    both the parent and, where relevant, any subsidiaries.

Our approach to risk

We recognise that the primary role of risk management is to help Our enterprise-wide risk appetite is expressed holistically through
protect our customers, business, colleagues, shareholders and the various risk management mechanisms and activities, in both
communities that we serve, while ensuring we are able to support our quantitative and qualitative terms and is formally articulated through our
strategy and provide sustainable growth. Risk Appetite Statement (‘RAS’).

In addition, we recognise the importance of a strong culture, which The Group’s risk appetite is established considering:
refers to our shared attitudes, beliefs, values and standards that shape
behaviours including those related to risk awareness, risk taking and – alignment with our strategy, purpose, values, external risk
risk management. All our people are responsible for the management environment, reputational and customer needs;
of risk, with ultimate supervisory oversight residing with the Board. – compliance with applicable laws, regulations and regulatory
The implementation of our business strategy remains a key focus. As – priorities;
we deliver change initiatives, we seek to actively manage the execution forward-looking insights into future risk exposure;
risks. We also perform periodic risk assessments, including against – sufficiency of available capital, liquidity and balance sheet leverage
strategies, to help ensure retention of key personnel for our continued to absorb the risks;
safe operation. – capacity and capabilities of people to manage the risk landscape;
        – functionality, capacity and resilience of available systems to manage
Our risk management framework – the risk landscape;
        effectiveness of the applicable control environment to mitigate risk;
We aim to use a comprehensive risk management approach across the and
organisation and across all risk types, underpinned by our culture and – internally and externally disclosed commitments.
values. This is outlined in our Risk Management Framework (‘RMF’), Performance against the Group’s RAS is reported to the Group Risk
including the key principles and practices that we employ in managing Management Meeting to support targeted insight and discussion of
material risks, both financial and non-financial. breaches of risk appetite and any associated mitigating actions. This
The RMF sets out in a consistent way how we identify, assess and reporting helps risks to be promptly identified and mitigated and
manage the risks that matter the most with respect to our ability to informs risk-adjusted remuneration to drive a strong risk culture.
operate, grow, and meet external commitments. It translates our Each principal subsidiary and material operating entity is covered by a
strategy, values and commitments into practical actions and risk-based RAS, and their alignment with the Group’s RAS is monitored.
decisions.

Our Group Risk and Compliance function is responsible for the Group’s Our risk governance
RMF. Independent from the business segments, including our sales
and trading functions, it provides challenge, oversight and appropriate The Board has ultimate supervisory responsibility for the effective
balance of risk and reward in decision-making. Its responsibility includes management of risk.
establishing global policy, monitoring risk profiles, and identifying and
managing forward-looking risk. The Group Chief Risk and Compliance Officer (‘GCRCO’), supported by
Our people are responsible for managing both financial and non- members of the Group Risk Management Meeting, holds executive
financial risk, including regulatory compliance and financial crime risks. accountability for the ongoing monitoring, assessment and
They are required to manage the risks of the business and operational management of the risk environment and the effectiveness of the risk
activities for which they are responsible. We maintain adequate management framework.
oversight of our risks through our various specialist risk stewards and The GCRCO is also responsible for the oversight of reputational risk,
the collective accountability held by our chief risk officers (‘CROs’) and with the support of the Group Reputational Risk Committee. Further
chief risk and compliance officers (‘CRCOs’). We seek to maintain a details can be found under the ‘Reputational risk’ section of
sound control environment and regularly test and monitor our www.hsbc.com/who-we-are/esg-and-responsible-business/managing-
controls, which aim to prevent risks from materialising, detect when risk.
they do, and recover and learn from issues in a timely manner within
our risk appetite. Day-to-day responsibility for risk management is delegated to senior
        managers with individual accountability for decision making.

        appropriate oversight and accountability of risk, which facilitates

to take to achieve our strategic objectives. structure is summarised in the following table.

help ensure it remains fit for purpose.

    Governance structure for the management of risk and compliance
     Authority                       Membership                           Responsibilities include:
     Group Risk Management           GCRCO                                –    Supporting the GCRCO in exercising Board-delegated risk management authority
     Meeting                         Group Chief Legal Officer            –    Overseeing the implementation of risk appetite and the risk management framework
                                     Group CEO                            –    Forward-looking assessment of the risk environment, analysing possible risk impacts
                                     Group CFO                                 and taking appropriate action
                                     All other Group Operating Committee

    banking operations, and these are covered by the Group’s risk management processes. However, there are specific risks inherent to the insurance
    operations as noted below.

    Implementation
    A centralised impairment engine calculates expected credit losses
    using data from various systems, which is subject to validation checks
    and enhancements from a variety of client, finance and risk systems.
    Where possible, these checks and processes are performed in a
    globally consistent and centralised manner.

    1   Customer risk rating (‘CRR’).

     Quality classification definitions
     –  ‘Strong’ exposures demonstrate a strong capacity to meet financial commitments, with negligible or low probability of default and/or low levels of
        expected loss.
     –  ‘Good’ exposures require closer monitoring and demonstrate a good capacity to meet financial commitments, with low default risk.
     –  ‘Satisfactory’ exposures require closer monitoring and demonstrate an average-to-fair capacity to meet financial commitments, with moderate default risk.
     –  ‘Sub-standard’ exposures require varying degrees of special attention and default risk is of greater concern.
     –  ‘Credit-impaired’ exposures have been assessed as described in Note 1.2(j) to the financial statements.

    The following table provides an overview of the Group’s credit risk by stage and industry, and the associated ECL coverage. The financial assets recorded in each stage have the following characteristics:

    –  Stage 1: These financial assets are unimpaired and without a significant increase in credit risk for which a 12-month allowance for ECL is recognised.
    –  Stage 2: A significant increase in credit risk has been experienced on these financial assets since initial recognition for which a lifetime ECL is recognised.
    –  Stage 3: There is objective evidence of impairment and the financial assets are therefore considered to be in default or otherwise credit impaired for which a lifetime ECL is recognised.
    –  Purchased or originated credit-impaired financial assets (‘POCI’): Financial assets that are purchased or originated at a deep discount are seen to reflect the incurred credit losses on which a lifetime ECL is
       recognised.

    Measurement uncertainty and sensitivity analysis of ECL estimates
    (Audited)
    The recognition and measurement of ECL involves the use of                   The consensus outer scenarios represent short-term cyclical deviations
    significant judgement and estimation. We form multiple scenarios             from the Central scenario, where variable paths converge back to long-
    based on economic forecasts and distributional estimates and apply           term trend expectations. They are calibrated to a 10% probability.
    these to credit risk models to estimate future credit losses. The results
    are then probability-weighted to determine an unbiased ECL estimate.         HSBC’s Central scenario assumes that the effects of announced
                                                                                 climate measures, carbon pricing and green levies are incorporated into
    Management assessed the current economic environment, reviewed               economic forecasts where their short-term effects are known from
    the latest economic forecasts and discussed key risks before selecting       enacted legislation, or may be reasonably projected from current trends
    economic scenarios and their weightings.                                     and statutory targets. Variable paths and projections aligned to long-
    Management judgemental adjustments are used where modelled                   term climate outcomes, but which are dependent on additional policy
    allowance for ECL does not fully reflect the identified risks and related    adjustments, carry greater uncertainty. Further details about climate
    uncertainty, or to capture significant late-breaking events.                 scenarios may be found in the ‘Insights from climate scenario analysis’
                                                                                 section of our Risk review on page 173.
    Methodology                                                                  The Downside 2 explores a more extreme economic outcome than
    At 31 December 2025, four economic scenarios were used to capture            those captured by the consensus scenarios. In this scenario, variables
    the latest economic expectations and to articulate management’s view         do not, by design, revert to long-term trend expectations and may
    of the range of risks and potential outcomes. Scenarios are created          instead explore alternative states of equilibrium, where economic
    using the latest economic forecasts and distributional estimates, each       variables move permanently away from past trends. It is calibrated to a
    quarter.                                                                     5% probability.
    Three scenarios, the Upside, Central and Downside, are drawn from            In most circumstances, the alignment of weightings with the calibrated
    external consensus forecasts, market data and distributional estimates       probability of scenarios is deemed appropriate for the unbiased
    of the entire range of economic outcomes. These estimates are used           estimation of ECL. However, management may depart from this
    as conditioning assumptions in a modelled expansion of other                 probability-based scenario weighting approach when the economic
    variables, to ensure scenarios that are economically coherent and            outlook and forecasts are determined to be particularly uncertain and
    internally consistent. The fourth scenario, the Downside 2, represents       risks are elevated.
    management’s view of severe downside risks.

    The consensus Central scenario is deemed the ‘most likely’ scenario,
    and will attract the largest probability weighting.

    Downside scenarios                                                                The consensus Downside scenario
    Downside scenarios explore the intensification and crystallisation of             In the consensus Downside scenario, the effects of tariffs on the
    key risk themes and are modelled so that economic shocks drive                    global economy are worse than expected, leading to weaker economic
    consumption and investment lower and commodity prices fall. For                   activity compared with the Central scenario. The scenario is consistent
    most markets, inflation and interest rates are lower compared with the            with the tariff rate, measured as an effective trade-weighted average,
    Central scenario. That narrative is disrupted in the US and Mexico as             rising to 19% in 2026, and remaining at that level in 2027. The key
    higher tariff rates and other countermeasures are assumed to drive a              driver of that increase is the application of sector-specific tariff rates.
    broad increase in import prices.                                                  In this scenario, GDP declines and unemployment rates rise, while
    Key downside risks include:                                                       asset prices and commodity prices fall. The scenario features an
    –  an increase in protectionist policies. This lowers investment,                 escalation in geopolitical tensions and an increase in tariffs over and
       complicates international supply chains, and impedes trade flows;              above those assumed in the Central scenario. Existing and recently
    –  abrupt asset repricing given elevated valuations, particularly in the          approved trade agreements are assumed to hold. In most markets,
       tech sector, eroding wealth effects and ultimately increasing credit           inflation declines relative to the Central scenario, as tariffs are assumed
       risks;                                                                         to drive a drop in export demand from the US. In the US and Mexico,
                                                                                      the scenario sees inflation rise as higher tariffs across a broad range of
    –      broader and more prolonged conflict in the Middle East and the             imported goods pass through to consumer prices.
       Russia-Ukraine war, which undermine confidence and investment;
       and                                                                            In the scenario, oil prices trough at $40 per barrel.
    –        continued differences between the US and China, which affect             The following tables describe key macroeconomic variables in the
       economic confidence and the global goods trade and supply chains               consensus Downside scenario.
       for critical technologies.

    Downside 2 scenario
    The Downside 2 scenario reflects management’s view of the tail of the                    most significant price adjustments. The scenario is consistent with the
    economic distribution. It incorporates the simultaneous crystallisation                  US tariff rate, measured as an effective trade-weighted average, rising
    of a number of risks that lead to a deep global recession. The                   to 25% in 2026, and remaining at that level in 2027.
    subsequent drop in demand leads to a steep fall in commodity prices,
    and a rapid increase in unemployment.                                            In the scenario, oil prices trough at $30 per barrel.

Scenario weighting
Scenario weightings are calibrated to probabilities that are determined         Consequently, there was no variation in scenario weights and they
with reference to consensus forecast probability distributions.                 were aligned to the calibrated probabilities of the scenarios. The
Management may then choose to vary weights if they assess that the              consensus Central scenario was assigned a 75% probability weighting
calibration lags more recent events, or does not reflect their view of          in our major markets. The consensus Upside scenario was assigned a
the distribution of economic and geopolitical risk. Management’s view           10% weighting, and the consensus Downside scenario was given
of the scenarios and the probability distribution takes into consideration      10%. The Downside 2 was assigned a 5% weighting.
the relationship of the consensus scenario to both internal and external
assessments of risk.                                                            In light of the US intervention in the political leadership and energy
                                                                                assets of Venezuela during early January 2026, management assessed
For the fourth quarter of 2025, forecast and distributional estimates           the potential implications, including to oil prices, and concluded that
were assessed to have incorporated available information around tariffs         expected spillovers remain within the scope of existing scenarios,
and policy uncertainties and no major events had occurred since                 including potentially significantly lower oil prices. Subsequent tariff
scenario production that changed the outlook materially. Forecast               developments in relation to Greenland were also assessed on the
dispersion, financial market volatility and other measures of uncertainty       same basis and no additional action was deemed necessary for
remained close to their long-term average.                                      economic scenarios or weights.

How economic scenarios are reflected in                                         Management judgemental adjustments
ECL calculations                                                                IFRS 9 management judgemental adjustments are typically short-term
Models are used to reflect economic scenarios for the ECL estimates.            increases or decreases to the modelled allowance for ECL at a
We have developed globally consistent methodologies for the                     customer, segment or portfolio level where management believes
application of forward economic guidance into the calculation of ECL            allowances do not sufficiently reflect the ECL at the reporting date.
for wholesale and retail credit risk.                                           These relate to risks or uncertainties that are not reflected in the
                                                                                models or to any late-breaking events with significant uncertainty,
For wholesale portfolios, a global methodology is used for the                  subject to management review and challenge.
estimation of the term structure of probability of default (‘PD’) and loss      Management judgemental adjustments impacts are considered for
given default (‘LGD’). PDs use the correlation of forward economic              both gross balances and allowances for ECL when determining
guidance with default rates for a particular industry within a country,         whether a significant increase in credit risk has occurred, and is
and LGDs use the correlation of forward economic guidance with                  allocated to an appropriate stage in accordance with the internal
collateral values and realisation rates for a particular country and            adjustments framework.
industry. PDs and LGDs are estimated for the entire term structure of
each instrument.                                                                Management judgemental adjustments are reviewed under the IFRS 9
                                                                                governance process see page 107. Management’s review and
For impaired loans, allowances for ECL estimates are based on                   challenge focuses on the rationale and adjustment amounts and,
discounted cash flow (‘DCF’) calculations for internal forward-looking          where significant, is subject to a further review by the second line of
scenarios specific to individual borrower circumstances. Probability-           defence. Internal frameworks establish the conditions where some
weighted outcomes are applied and, depending on materiality and the             management judgemental adjustments should no longer be required
status of the borrower, the number of scenarios considered will                 and as such are considered as part of the governance process.
change. Where relevant for the case being assessed, forward
economic guidance is considered as part of these scenarios. LGD-                The internal governance process regularly reviews management
driven ECL estimates are used for certain less material cases.                  judgemental adjustments and, where possible, mitigates these through
                                                                                a model recalibration or redevelopment.
For our retail portfolios, the models are predominantly based on
historical observations and correlations with default rates and collateral      Management judgemental adjustment drivers evolve as the economic
values.                                                                         environment changes and new risks emerge. In addition to
                                                                                management judgemental adjustments there are also ‘Other
For PD, the impact of economic scenarios is modelled for each                   adjustments’, which are made to address process limitations and data/
portfolio, using historical relationships between default rates and             model deficiencies and can also include, where appropriate, the impact
macroeconomic variables. These are included within IFRS 9 ECL                   of new models where governance has sufficiently progressed to allow
estimates using either economic response models or models that                  an accurate estimate of ECL allowance to be incorporated into the total
contain internal, external and macroeconomic variables. The                     reported ECL.

     Scenarios
     100% Consensus Central scenario                                                         (0.0)         0.0   (0.1)                                              (0.2)

    1  On the same basis as retail and wholesale sensitivity analysis.

                         At 31 December 2025, the Group allowance for ECL increased in the retail portfolio by $0.3bn and decreased by $0.3bn in the wholesale portfolio,
    compared with 31 December 2024.

                               Compared with 31 December 2024, both the retail and wholesale portfolio Group ECL sensitivity across all consensus scenarios decreased due
    to an improving economic outlook. For the retail portfolios the ECL sensitivity decrease across the Downside 2 scenario was primarily due to
    improvements in Hong Kong and UK unsecured portfolios. For the wholesale portfolios, the decrease was largely driven by crystallisation of
    defaults in certain sectors and an improving economic outlook.

    Credit quality
    Credit quality of financial instruments
    (Audited)
    We assess the credit quality of all financial instruments that are subject to credit risk. The credit quality of financial instruments is a point-in-time
               assessment of PD, whereas stages 1 and 2 are determined based on relative deterioration of credit quality since initial recognition for the majority
    of portfolios. Accordingly, for non-credit-impaired financial instruments, there is no direct relationship between the credit quality assessment and
    stages 1 and 2, although typically the lower credit quality bands exhibit a higher proportion in stage 2. The five credit quality classifications provided
    below each encompass a range of granular internal credit rating grades assigned to wholesale and personal lending businesses and the external
    ratings attributed by external agencies to debt securities, as shown in the table on page 139.

    Wholesale lending

    The table below provides a breakdown by industry sector and stage of the Group’s gross carrying amount and allowances for ECL for wholesale
    loans and advances to banks and customers. Counterparties or exposures are classified when presenting comparable economic characteristics, or
    engaged in similar activities so that their collective ability to meet contractual obligations is uniformly affected by changes in economic, political or
    other conditions. Therefore, the industry classification does not adhere to Nomenclature des Activités Économiques dans la Communauté
    Européenne, which is applicable to other financial regulatory reporting.

                                                                          During the year, there was a net transfer from stage 2 to stage 1 of $7,602m gross carrying/nominal amounts. This was mainly driven by model
                                                                          recalibration for retail portfolios in HSBC UK ($11,245m) where the PD was aligned to the most recent observed performance. This was partly
                                                                           offset by a net transfer from stage 1 to stage 2 in Hong Kong ($1,152m) primarily due to a new mortgage model implementation and in Mexico
    ($1,042m) within the unsecured lending portfolios.

    publication of wide-ranging information on their risks, capital and                      At 31 December 2025, all of the Group’s material operating entities
    management.                                                                              were above the required regulatory minimum liquidity and funding
    Ñ For further details, see our Pillar 3 Disclosures at 31 December 2025, which           levels. Each entity maintains sufficient unencumbered liquid assets to

    Sources of funding
    Our primary sources of funding are customer current accounts and                   The funding risk management framework seeks to ensure operating
    savings deposits payable on demand or at short notice. We issue                    entities maintain a diversified funding profile defined in their funding
    secured and unsecured wholesale securities to supplement customer                    plans which are taken through regular governance, in line with globally
    deposits, meet regulatory obligations and seek to ensure that we                    consistent policies and standards. Diversification is achieved through a
    maintain a diversified funding profile through a balanced mix of                   balanced mix of funding sources, tenors, currencies and geographies,

                                         Structural foreign exchange risk in 2025
    Structural foreign exchange exposures represent net assets or capital investments in subsidiaries, branches, joint arrangements or associates,
                                                                     together with any associated hedges, the functional currencies of which are currencies other than the US dollar. Exchange differences on
    structural exposures are usually recognised in ‘other comprehensive income’.

    Interest rate risk in the banking book in 2025
    Banking net interest income sensitivity
    Banking NII sensitivity is the sensitivity of our banking net interest                              As the market and policy rates move, the degree to which these
    income to interest rate shocks. This metric includes the sensitivity            changes are passed on to customers will vary based on several factors,
    arising from the use of banking book liabilities to fund trading assets, as     including the absolute level of market interest rates, regulatory and
    well as the impacts of vanilla foreign exchange swaps to optimise cash          contractual frameworks, and competitive dynamics. To aid
    management across the Group. It is aligned with the presentation in                                 comparability between markets, we have simplified the basis of
    the Group’s financial disclosures of banking NII as an alternative              preparation for our disclosure and have used a 50% pass-on
    performance measure intended to approximate the Group’s banking                         assumption for major entities on certain interest-bearing deposits. Our
    revenue that is directly impacted by changes in interest rates.                                   asset pass-on assumptions are largely in line with our contractual
    The following tables set out the assessed impact to a hypothetical                      agreements or established market practice, which typically results in a
    base case projection of our banking NII under an immediate shock of             significant portion of interest rate changes being passed on.

    years). For example, Year 3 shows the impact of an immediate rate               decrease projected banking NII by $3.4bn.
    shock on the banking NII projected for the third year.                          The sensitivity of banking NII for the 12 months as at 31 December

    1  Portfolio diversification is the market risk dispersion effect of holding a portfolio containing different risk types. It represents the reduction in unsystematic
          market risk that occurs when combining a number of different risk types – such as interest rate and credit spreads – together in one portfolio. It is measured as
       the difference between the sum of the VaR by individual risk type and the combined total VaR. A negative number represents the benefit of portfolio
       diversification. As the maximum and minimum occurs on different days for different risk types, it is not meaningful to calculate a portfolio diversification benefit
       for these measures.
    2  The total VaR is non-additive across risk types due to diversification effects.

    Climate risk TCFD

    Our climate risk approach identifies two primary drivers of climate risk:     encounter climate risks directly or indirectly through our customer
    –  physical risk, which arises from the increased frequency and               relationships, potentially leading to both financial and non-financial
       severity of extreme weather events, such as hurricanes and floods,         consequences.
       or chronic gradual shifts in weather patterns or rises in the sea level;   Our climate risk approach aims to effectively manage the material risks
       and                                                                        that could impact our operations, financial performance and stability,
    –  transition risk, which arises from the process of moving to a net          and reputation. It is informed by the evolving expectations of our
       zero economy, including changes in government policy and                   regulators and is aligned to our Group-wide risk management
       legislation, technology, market demand, and reputational                   framework, which sets out how we identify, assess and manage our
       implications triggered by a change in stakeholder expectations,            risks across our three lines of defence.
       action or inaction.                                                        We continue to work to enhance our climate risk capabilities across our
    We continue to identify a thematic issue related to climate risk that         businesses by prioritising sectors, portfolios and counterparties with
    could manifest as reputational, regulatory compliance, and litigation         the highest impacts. Recognising this as a long-term iterative process,
    risks: the risk of greenwashing. This risk arises from knowingly or           we aim to expand our coverage and integrate more advanced data,
    unknowingly making inaccurate, unclear, misleading or unsubstantiated         climate analytics, frameworks and tools, while adapting to emerging
    claims regarding sustainability to our stakeholders.                          industry best practices and climate-related regulations.
    Net zero alignment risk had previously been identified as a thematic          We regularly reflect on the evolving nature of financial and non-financial
    issue and is now replaced and managed within the new risk type,               climate risks in the real world to improve the integration of climate risk
    sustainability execution risk.                                                factors into strategic planning, transactions, and decision-making
                                                                                  across our operations. Our current processes for managing climate and
    Ñ See page 105 for our definition of climate risk.                            sustainability-related targets, net zero transition plans, and climate
                                                                                  strategy include conducting impact assessments of HSBC's M&A
    Approach                                                                      activities.

1  Amounts shown in the table also include green and other sustainable finance loans, which support the transition to the net zero economy. The methodology for
   quantifying our exposure to high transition risk sectors and the transition risk metrics will evolve over time as more data becomes available and is incorporated
   into our risk management systems and processes. We are aiming to develop the appropriate systems, data and processes to provide enhanced disclosures in
   future years.
2  Counterparties are allocated to the high transition risk sectors via a two-step approach. Firstly, where the main business of a group of connected counterparties
   is in a high transition risk sector, all lending to the group is included in one high transition risk sector irrespective of the sector of each individual obligor within
   the group. Secondly, where the main business of a group of connected counterparties is not in a high transition risk sector, only lending to individual obligors in
   the high transition risk sectors is included. The main business of a group of connected counterparties is identified by the industry that generates the majority of
   revenue within a group. Customer revenue data utilised during this allocation process is the most recent and readily available and will not always align to our
   own reporting period.
3  The six high transition risk sectors make up 17.5% of the total gross carrying amount of wholesale loans and advances to banks and customers of $635bn.
   Amounts include assets held for sale.
4  The sectors used to monitor the wholesale corporate lending portfolio set out in the table are different to the scope of sectors we focus on for financed
   emissions targets and reporting. The latter focus on the most carbon-emissive sectors, and the parts of the value chain where we believe the majority of
   emissions are produced to help reduce double counting. These sectors are set out within the 'Financed emissions' section on page 41.

Traded risk                                                                   Financial reporting risk
Climate risk may result in trading losses due to increases in market          Climate risk impacts financial reporting risk through increased
volatility and widening spreads from the macro and microeconomic              disclosure requirements.
impacts of transition and physical risk. We monitor climate sensitive
exposures against regional and global limits in our global and entity         The scope of financial reporting risk includes oversight of the accuracy
mandates, including for vulnerable countries and high-transition risk         and completeness of ESG and climate-related reporting. Our risk
sectors.                                                                      appetite statement states that HSBC has no appetite for material errors

    1  Carbon price represents the cost effects of climate-related policies that aim to discourage carbon-emitting activities and encourage low-carbon solutions. The
                                                   expected result of higher carbon prices is a reduction in emissions as high emissions become uneconomical.
    2  The scenario characteristics shown for the Severe Climate Stress scenario only describe the scenario that was used to assess credit risk.
    3  Representative Concentration Pathways (RCPs) are a set of greenhouse gas concentration trajectories developed for climate modelling and research. They were
       formally adopted by the IPCC and are used to assess the potential impacts of climate change based on different levels of greenhouse gas emissions.

    We have designed a suite of diverse climate scenarios that explore                                              To support how we assess the climate-related impacts observed

    The climate scenarios are underpinned by well-established industry                                              entailed taking our Current Commitments scenario and removing the
    bodies, such as the Network for Greening the Financial System                                                   climate impacts, using climate-related GDP deviations as a proxy.
    (‘NGFS’) Phase V, the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change                                                 The Severe Climate Stress scenario is a highly improbable short- and
    (‘IPCC’) and International Energy Agency (‘IEA’), which are further                                             medium-term stress scenario, aligned to NGFS’s Short-Term Scenario

    There are three long-term scenarios. The Below 2 Degrees scenario is                                            transition to net zero. It has the effect of compressing both physical

    Commitments scenario to support the Group’s financial planning, as                                              is extreme and highly unlikely, it assists us in understanding our current
    this is deemed to be the most likely scenario to occur over the five-                                           exposures.
    year planning horizon. The Downside Physical Risk scenario is a less
    probable scenario with higher global warming and more significant
    physical risk impacts.

        to take measures to help ensure business continuity in affected markets
        should the situation require. When disruptive events occur, businesses
        and infrastructure functions continually review their continuity plans and
        response to minimise any potential impacts.

    Credit risk                                                                     The principal risk we face is that, over time, the cost of the contract,
    (Audited)                                                                       including claims and benefits, may exceed the total amount of
                                                                                    premiums and investment income received.
    Description and exposure                                                        The tables on page 183 analyse our life insurance underwriting risk

    Insurance underwriting risk is the risk of loss through adverse
    experience, in either timing or amount, of insurance underwriting
    parameters (non-economic assumptions). These parameters include
    mortality, morbidity, longevity, lapse and expense rates.

    Board and senior management diversity
    We value difference
    We believe that a diverse and inclusive Board, reflective of the communities we serve, is a
    critical component of effective decision-making and of developing a sustainable and
    successful business for HSBC.

   How we are governed


    We are committed to high standards of corporate governance. The Group has in place a comprehensive range of policies and procedures to help
    ensure that its end-to-end governance is well managed, with appropriate and effective oversight and controls.

     Board of Directors         The Board has overall, collective responsibility for the long-term success of the Group and delivery of sustainable value to
                                shareholders. Led by the Group Chairman, the Board is responsible for, among other matters:
     A schedule of matters       –  approving the Group’s strategy and objectives, and monitoring the alignment of the Group’s purpose, strategy and values with the
     reserved for the Board is      desired culture and standards;
 set out within its terms of – setting the Group’s risk appetite and monitoring the Group’s risk profile;
     reference, which are        –  approving and monitoring capital and financial resource plans for achieving strategic objectives, including material transactions;
     available at                –  considering and approving the Group’s technology and environmental, social and governance strategies;
 www.hsbc.com/who-we- – reviewing the effectiveness of stakeholder engagement mechanisms, including engagement with the workforce;
     are/our-people/board-of-    –  approving appointments to the Board and Board roles, and the remuneration of independent non-executive Directors;
     directors/board-            –  reviewing and approving changes to the Group’s overall corporate governance arrangements; and
     responsibilities.           –  providing entrepreneurial leadership of the Group within a framework of prudent and effective controls, which enable risks to be
                                    assessed and managed.

    The Board delegates oversight of certain matters to its committees, which are each chaired by a non-executive Director. Board committees provide
    regular reports on their activities and make recommendations to the Board. Only the Group Chairman and non-executive Directors are members of
    Board committees. Details of committee memberships are in the Directors' biographies in 'The Board' section on pages 187 to 190. Terms of
    reference of the Board committees are available at www.hsbc.com/who-we-are/our-people/board-of-directors/board-committees.

     Chairman’s Committee       An ad hoc committee which provides Board members with the opportunity to consider time-critical matters between scheduled Board
                                meetings.
     Group Audit Committee      Oversees matters relating to the Group’s internal controls, financial resourcing and reporting, internal and external audit, and
     ('GAC')                    whistleblowing arrangements. For more information, see the Committee’s report from page 203.
     Nomination & Corporate     Oversees Board and senior management succession planning and monitors the corporate governance framework of the Group. For
     Governance Committee       more information, see the Committee’s report from page 200.
     Group Remuneration         Responsible for reviewing and making recommendations to the Board, for approval by shareholders, on the Group remuneration policy
     Committee                  and approving the remuneration of the executive Directors and other senior employees. For more information, see the committee’s
                                report from page 216.
     Group Risk Committee       Oversees and advises the Board on all risk-related matters, including financial and non-financial risks. For more information, see the
     ('GRC')                    Committee’s report from page 209.
     Group Technology &         Oversees HSBC’s technology and operations strategies and monitors alignment with overall Group strategy. For more information, see
     Operations Committee       the Committee’s report from page 213.
     ('GTO')
     Other Governance           Board Oversight Sub-Group: an informal mechanism whereby a      Board Sustainability Working Group ('SWG'):    supports the
     Forums                     smaller group of Board members and management may meet on       delivery of the sustainability strategy and provides oversight and
                                an ad hoc basis to discuss emerging issues and upcoming Board   guidance in relation to the Group’s sustainability activities. The
                                matters. This Sub-Group is chaired by the Group Chairman.       SWG is comprised of four non-executive Directors. Further
                                                                                                information about SWG activities is on page 58.

    The Board delegates day-to-day management of the business and delivery of strategy to the Group CEO. During the year, the Group CEO was
    supported in these responsibilities by the Group Operating Committee ('Group OpCo').

     Group Operating            Supports the Group CEO in the day-to-day management of the Group. Comprised of 12 members of senior management including
     Committee (Group           infrastructure heads and the CEOs of each of our four business areas. The Group OpCo members are set out on page 191. The Group
     OpCo)                      OpCo operates under written terms of reference and its members report to the Board on matters as appropriate.

    A number of committees support the Group OpCo by providing specialist oversight and guidance of the matters delegated to them. Such
    matters include restructuring and investment considerations, risk management and controls, financial reporting and disclosures.

     Group Finance Management            Group Risk Management      Group Disclosure Committee                             Acquisitions and Disposals
